SVD Pep Talk
Singular value decomposition (SVD) is an important concept in linear algebra.
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
We assume you have taken (or are taking) a linear algebra course.
We will not explain SVD in its entirety-in particular, we will not prove:
o Why SVD is a valid decomposition of rectangular matrices, and
· Why PCA is an application of SVD.
Today, we will only cover what is needed for PCA.
Checkout the EE16 notes for a deeper study of SVD:
https://eecs16b.org/notes/sp24/note15.pdf

SVD Pep Talk
Singular value decomposition (SVD) is an important concept in linear algebra.
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
We assume you have taken (or are taking) a linear algebra course.
We will not explain SVD in its entirety-in particular, we will not prove:
o Why SVD is a valid decomposition of rectangular matrices, and
· Why PCA is an application of SVD.
Today, we will only cover what is needed for PCA.
Checkout the EE16 notes for a deeper study of SVD:
https://eecs16b.org/notes/sp24/note15.pdf

Important Note on Centring and Standardizing the Data
y Across dimensions is meaning kil
When running PCA it is important to center the data (ensure data is centered at 0):
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
X = X - np.mean(X, axis = 0)
· The Scikit-Learn PCA code does this automatically
You should also consider standardizing your data (ensure unit variance on each dimension)
X = (X - np.mean(X, axis = 0)) / np.std(X, axis = 0)
The Scikit-Learn PCA code does not do this
You should standardize your data if features have vastly different ranges
You should not standardize your data if the units are all the same and the differences in
variability across dimensions is meaningful (e.g ., everything is in the same units)

Important Note on Centring and Standardizing the Data
E
y ucioas dimensione Is meaning idil
When running PCA it is important to center the data (ensure data is centered at 0):
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
X = X - np.mean(X, axis = 0)
·
The Scikit-Learn PCA code does this automatically
You should also consider standardizing your data (ensure unit variance on each dimension)
X = (X - np.mean(X, axis = 0)) / np.std(X, axis = 0)
The Scikit-Learn PCA code does not do this
You should standardize your data if features have vastly different ranges
You should not standardize your data if the units are all the same and the differences in
variability across dimensions is meaningful (e.g ., everything is in the same units)

U, S, Vt = np. linalg. svd(X, full_matrices = False)
## Construct the latent factors
Z = U[ :, : k] @ np.diag(S[:k])
## Reconstruct the original rectangle using the factors Z and the principle components
rectangle_hat = pd. DataFrame(Z @ Vt [ : k, : ], columns = rectangle. columns)
## Scale and shift the factors back to the original coordinate system
rectangle_hat = rectangle_hat * np.std( rectangle, axis = 0) + np.mean( rectangle, axis = 0)

[ ]: k = 2
U, S, Vt = np. linalg. svd(X, full_matrices = False)
## Construct the latent factors
Z = U[ :, : k] @ np.diag(S[:k])
## Reconstruct the original rectangle using the factors Z and the principle components
rectangle_hat = pd. DataFrame(Z @ Vt [ : k, : ], columns = rectangle. columns)
## Scale and shift the factors back to the original coordinate system
rectangle_hat = rectangle_hat * np.std( rectangle, axis = 0) + np.mean( rectangle, axis = 0)

Congressional Vote Records
Let's examine how the House of Representatives (of the 116th Congress, 1st session) voted in the month of September 2019.
From the U.S. Senate website:
Roll call votes occur when a representative or senator votes "yea" or "nay," so that the names of members voting on each side are recorded. A voice vote
is a vote in which those in favor or against a measure say "yea" or "nay," respectively, without the names or tallies of members voting on each side being
recorded.
-
The data, compiled from ProPublica source, is a "skinny" table of data where each record is a single vote by a member across any roll call in the 116th Congress, 1st
session, as downloaded in February 2020. The member ofthe House, whom we'll call legislator, is denoted by their bioguide alphanumeric ID in
http://bioguide.congress.gov/.

Congressional Vote Records
Let's examine how the House of Representatives (of the 116th Congress, 1st session) voted in the month of September 2019.
From the U.S. Senate website:
Roll call votes occur when a representative or senator votes "yea" or "nay," so that the names of members voting on each side are recorded. A voice vote
is a vote in which those in favor or against a measure say "yea" or "nay," respectively, without the names or tallies of members voting on each side being
recorded.
-
The data, compiled from ProPublica source, is a "skinny" table of data where each record is a single vote by a member across any roll call in the 116th Congress, 1st
session, as downloaded in February 2020. The member ofthe House, whom we'll call legislator, is denoted by their bioguide alphanumeric ID in
http://bioguide.congress.gov/.
[ ]:
# February 2019 House of Representatives roll call votes

Congressional Vote Records
Let's examine how the House of Representatives (of the 116th Congress, 1st session) voted in the month of September 2019.
From the U.S. Senate website:
Roll call votes occur when a representative or senator votes "yea" or "nay," so that the names of members voting on each side are recorded. A voice vote
is a vote in which those in favor or against a measure say "yea" or "nay," respectively, without the names or tallies of members voting on each side being
recorded.
-
The data, compiled from ProPublica source, is a "skinny" table of data where each record is a single vote by a member across any roll call in the 116th Congress, 1st
session, as downloaded in February 2020. The member ofthe House, whom we'll call legislator, is denoted by their bioguide alphanumeric ID in
http://bioguide.congress.gov/.

Component Scores
If the first two singular values are large and all others are small, then two dimensions are enough to describe most of what distinguishes one observation from another.
If not, then a PCA scatter plot is omitting lots of information.
An equivalent way to evaluate this is to determine the variance ratios, i.e ., the fraction of the variance each PC contributes to total variance.

Component Scores
If the first two singular values are large and all others are small, then two dimensions are enough to describe most of what distinguishes one observation from another.
If not, then a PCA scatter plot is omitting lots of information.
An equivalent way to evaluate this is to determine the variance ratios, i.e ., the fraction of the variance each PC contributes to total variance.

Audio: We take each of our principal components. and make a table actually the picture that's going back to slides for a second. We did this already. Oh, by fly. Okay. So if I take my v matrix, these are my principal components. So this is principal component 1, principal component 2, and so on. And I look at each row of this. It corresponds to one of the bills. And it's how much that bill positively voting positively or negatively contributes that component. Which means I can plot... a vector for each one of these entries so this is a vector for bill one a vector for bill two and so on. Because it's two numbers, right? So I have the number one, number one, principle point of value one, principle point of value two. I'll do one here. So principle point one, principle point two, the corresponding value. Those I can plot like this in my plot. And they show me for each bill which direction it's pointing. Is that Bill Moore lining with the main, the first prince, point, Axis, the second or the combination of the two? Let's go back to our code. So this is the first principal opponent, second principal opponent, Vectors. And this is the corresponding bill. And so this is a vector right here in my principal component plot. Fashion-MNIST dataset
We will be using the Fashion-MNIST dataset, which is a cool little dataset with gray scale 28x28 images of articles of clothing.
Fashion-MNIST: a Novel Image Dataset for Benchmarking Machine Learning Algorithms. Han Xiao, Kashif Rasul, Roland Vollgraf. arXiv:1708.07747
https://github.com/zalandoresearch/fashion-mnist

Visualizing images
The following snippet of code visualizes the images
[ ]: def show_images (images, ncols=5, max_images=30):
# conver the subset of images into a n, 28, 28 matrix for facet visualization
img_mat = np. array(images. head(max_images) ['images ' ] .to_list ( ) )
fig = px. imshow (img_mat, color_continuous_scale='gray',
facet_col = 0, facet_col_wrap=ncols,
height = 220*int(np.ceil(len(images)/ncols)))
fig. update_layout (coloraxis_showscale=False)
# Extract the facet number and convert it back to the class label.
fig. for_each_annotation(lambda a: a. update(text=images. iloc[int(a. text. split("=") [-1] ) ] ['class' ] ) )
return fig
show images ( images. head (20) )

Fashion-MNIST dataset
We will be using the Fashion-MNIST dataset, which is a cool little dataset with gray scale 28x28 images of articles of clothing.
Fashion-MNIST: a Novel Image Dataset for Benchmarking Machine Learning Algorithms. Han Xiao, Kashif Rasul, Roland Vollgraf. arXiv:1708.07747
https://github.com/zalandoresearch/fashion-mnist

Visualizing images
The following snippet of code visualizes the images
[ ]: def show_images (images, ncols=5, max_images=30):
# conver the subset of images into a n, 28, 28 matrix for facet visualization
img_mat = np. array(images. head(max_images) ['images ' ] .to_list ( ) )
fig = px. imshow (img_mat, color_continuous_scale='gray',
facet_col = 0, facet_col_wrap=ncols,
height = 220*int(np.ceil(len(images)/ncols)))
fig. update_layout (coloraxis_showscale=False)
# Extract the facet number and convert it back to the class label.
fig. for_each_annotation(lambda a: a. update(text=images. iloc[int(a. text. split("=") [-1] ) ] ['class' ] ) )
return fig
show images ( images. head (20) )

Audio: Visualize the first two. Hello mess! I can cover the data. Now we can see the different types of clothing, so trousers or adjacent to coats, interesting dresses. Dresses over here. Coats, T-shirts, and then what's over here? We've got sneakers, ankle boots, sandals, pullover, that's weird. Shoes and stuff, proper in this dimension. So now to see some structure in this data. Good hard dimensions. See the more structure. So I need three, maybe four or five dimensions to explain this data. to like, what are brighter images or darker images and see if it breaks down differently. But there is pretty clear structure, like ankle boots seem to live up here, sneakers, and shoes on this side of the axis, the side of the plot. I can start to see hey these jeans seem to cluster in how they are expressed in different parts of my plot. Now I might want to study these groups of jeans together. Right. All right, so this is how we use PCA for for data visualization. It is how we take high-dimensional data like images, votes and try to make sense of them in lower dimensions for visualization. And again, for some modeling techniques you might do this too, you might apply PCA to simplify your data. For linear models, it would not make sense to do that. I don't have enough time to explain why, but maybe you can think about it. So remember PCA is going to just project my data onto a matrix W and just some onto my principal components. Video: 1: File

Audio: And all my linear models are also projecting my data onto matrix. So Doing an additional matrix multiply may not do much. You can think about why that is. Leave that for an exercise. Alright. That is it for PCA. Starting next week, we have just one lecture and then we have Thanksgiving break. Next week's lecture is on clustering, which is another really fun algorithm for how we break our data into different groups. And then you get a break and we come back and you should all come to lecture. We're going to do neural nets for the first time and then we'll do large language models and I will explain how a chat to BT works in all of its detail. And so after that lecture, it will no longer be magic. So the last two lectures should be a lot of fun. Have a great Thanksgiving. I look forward to seeing a few of you on Tuesday and then hopefully the rest of you. The week after. Bye. Video: 1: File
